textDisplay: The section on thumb independence at 6:30 is exactly what I needed. I have been playing for 3 years and this is the piece that finally clicked for me. Thank you for showing it so slowly.
Those artificial harmonics in the bridge are so beautiful. I had no idea Tommy was doing that with his right hand thumb. Going to need to watch that section about 50 more times.

The harp harmonic technique is one of Tommy's most distinctive things. Lenny Breau was probably the original master of it on guitar. Check out my harmonics video (vid_007) for a dedicated breakdown of the technique.
I was in a Tommy workshop last year in Seattle and he showed us this exact technique live. Seeing it slowed down like this makes it so much more accessible.
Gerald can you post the tab for this? I am in your Thursday workshop and we tried this last week but I need to review the DADGAD section again.
Kenji I put the session recording up on Vimeo for you all - check the student link I sent. The tab is linked in the description there. See you Thursday.

Been playing Drifting for six months and still learning something new every watch. The two-handed tapping section here is the clearest explanation I have ever seen. The camera angle finally shows what his right hand index is doing.
The discussion of voice leading at 9:30 is genuinely insightful. Most guitar tutorials skip the theory and just show you where to put your fingers. This actually explains the harmonic reasoning.

I studied classical guitar for 10 years and only recently discovered fingerstyle players doing Bach. The tone is different but the musical content is identical. Really nice to see crossover content like this.
Kaki King is criminally underrated. People talk about Tommy Emmanuel and Andy McKee constantly but she is just as innovative if not more so. Great that this clip captures both hands so well.

I have been trying to learn Travis picking for a year from various sources and nothing has worked. 30 minutes into this video and I finally have the thumb going on its own. The exercise at 22:00 is the one.

Really glad that exercise helped. The key for most people is slowing it WAY down and drilling the thumb alone for a full week before adding the fingers. Patience with that stage pays off immediately after.

This is the most thorough DADGAD intro I have seen. The way you show the resonance of the open strings with the chord shapes makes the tuning make sense intuitively rather than just memorizing shapes.
I have been chasing that sound Tommy gets in his ballads forever. Harp harmonics - that is what it is called. Now I have a name for what I am trying to learn. Thank you.
The structural analysis of the D major section is excellent. Most people just play through it but understanding that it is the emotional center of the piece changes how you approach the dynamics.
Autumn Leaves is my favorite standard and I have wanted a proper chord melody version for fingerstyle for years. The walking bass under the chords in the bridge is exactly what I was missing.
I have watched this performance maybe 200 times over the years. Still incredible every time. The way he shifts between total control and wild abandon in the middle section is just unreal.
The section on integrating percussion with melody at 18:30 is gold. That is the hardest part - making it sound musical rather than just showing off. Really well explained.

Rylynn is the reason I started playing guitar seriously. Watching this full album performance made me realize how consistent Andy's technique is across a 50 minute set. The tuning stability alone is impressive.
The ornament choices in the Sarabande are so thoughtful. You can hear that you have actually thought about what serves the music rather than just copying a specific recording.

The nail shaping section at 16:30 is something nobody else talks about. I had an uneven break on my index nail and it was ruining my tone for months. Fixed the shape and it is a completely different sound.

I studied jazz piano for years and have been trying to bring that harmonic language to fingerstyle guitar. Drop 2 voicings are exactly the bridge I needed. This video just saved me months of figuring it out alone.
This is the greatest clip in guitar YouTube history. I have sent it to more people than any other video. The crowd reaction at the end says everything.

I took my Taylor to a tech after watching this and told him exactly what I wanted - lower action at the nut for single note clarity without losing projection. He said most players do not know how to describe what they want this specifically. The framing from this video helped.
Growing up in Nashville I saw Chet a few times before he passed. This lesson captures his essence better than most academic analyses I have read. The muted bass sound is everything.
This song has meant so much to me for a long time. The rubato section in this arrangement is beautiful - you can feel the breathing in the phrasing. Not just playing the notes.
I have been looking for a solo guitar version of White Winter Hymnal for years. Capo 4 makes so much sense. The way the harmony layers work on single guitar is really clever.

The explanation of why the cascading open strings work at 17:00 finally made me understand what Andy is doing harmonically. It is not random - each cascade implies a specific chord tone. Changed everything about how I hear this piece.
The spaced pair technique at 8:00 is giving me the sound I have been chasing for two years. My previous recordings always sounded flat and now I understand why - I was using a single mic dead center.
Lenny Breau is one of the great tragedies of music history. A man that brilliant dying so young. This tribute is respectful and the harmonic analysis is accurate without being dry.

The Open D section at 18:00 is exactly the sound I have heard on Celtic guitar recordings but never been able to recreate. Now I know why - I was in standard tuning and fighting the instrument the whole time.

The Bourree was the first piece of classical music I ever learned on guitar and I still love it. The ornament discussion at 20:00 is something my teacher never covered. Going to bring this to my next lesson.
I take the workshop at Dragonfly Music Center and this video is exactly what Gerald teaches us in session. Really nice to have a public version to share with friends who ask what the class covers.
We covered Travis picking in the beginner workshop last month. Revisiting this video now after practicing for three weeks - the difference is incredible. The slow work actually works.

I play jazz professionally with a pick and have been wanting to transition to fingerstyle for chord melody work. This is the clearest explanation of how chord melody works on acoustic guitar I have found.

The difference between snare simulation on the body vs the bridge is something I never noticed listening to Drifting. Going back to the recording now and I can hear exactly which one Andy uses when. Total game changer.
